straight_beat.name: Walt Brzezinski
straight_beat.column: The Tigers lost again. Not in a dramatic way, not with a last-minute rally or a walk-off home run, but in the kind of fashion that makes you want to rip up your scorecard and start over. Miami’s 8-2 victory yesterday wasn’t a masterpiece, but it wasn’t a mistake either. The Tigers, now 7-9, sit 2

optimist.name: Patsy Kwiatkowski
optimist.column:
The Tigers lost yesterday, but here’s the thing: they’re still in this thing. You know what’s more Detroit than a 7-9 record? A team that’s already planning a parade for a Cy Young winner. Skubal’s slider is the most Detroit thing I’ve seen since the Fox Theatre reopened. That pitch? It’s got the same kind of stubbornness that made our city stick around after the ’80s. You can’t teach that. You either have it or you don’t, and Skubal’s got it in spades.  

Look, the Marlins beat us, sure. But let’s not forget who we’re talking about here. This isn’t just another team with a young core. This is a roster built on grit, on the kind of grit that makes you want to throw a pancake at a wall and yell, “This is our house!” Skubal’s not just a pitcher; he’s a statement. Every time he takes the mound, he’s saying, “I’m here, and I’m not leaving until I’ve proven this city wrong.” And let’s be real—this city’s been wrong about a lot of things. But not about its spirit.  

The record? It’s a blip. A blip that’s still in the single digits. We’re not out of the race, and we’re not out of time. The AL Central’s a

pessimist.name: Nick Arbogast
pessimist.column: The Marlins handed us another lesson in how to lose a game. Not with a stunning collapse or a meltdown, but with the kind of quiet, methodical dominance that makes you wonder if we’ve been handed a playbook written by a team that’s already won the World Series.
